76212.subject: Agentic Payments on Avalanche: The Layer After Trust
76212.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.team1.blog/p/agentic-payments-on-avalanche-the

Identity tells an agent who a counterparty is. Reputation tells it whether to trust them. But the event that actually closes the loop is payment, and once agents pay each other hundreds of times an hour, the chain underneath stops being a detail.
Months ago I pointed an autonomous browser agent at Snowtrace.io to take a tedious data-collection job off my hands. It worked through the pages with a strange patience, clicking buttons and reading tooltips, slow but steady, until it froze on a dropdown menu and failed the task one step from the finish line.
So I asked our front-end team at Routescan for a small, non-invasive change to the interface the agent kept tripping over. They shipped the fix the next day. I reran the task, the agent cleared the dropdown, and it handed me the files I needed. A few days later it hit me what we had actually done: that commit was the first change we had ever shipped on Snowtrace for a machine instead of a human. I had spent months with our UX team on microcopy and user journeys, and almost without noticing we wrote our first iteration for machine experience instead of user experience.
That is a small story about a dropdown. It is also the leading edge of a larger reordering, and the part that gets the least attention is money.
Want to keep up with the latest in the Avalanche ecosystem? Subscribe to the Team1 blog and get our updates in your inbox weekly.
The Piece Everyone Skips
Two recent Team1 blog posts map this territory well.
Chris Fusillo of XKOVA framed ERC-8004 as a trust layer - focusing on identity, reputation, and validation to answer whether to trust an agent.
Meanwhile, Giacomo Barbieri (Jack) made the concept concrete by deploying an identity for his agent, Eva, on the C-Chain and pricing tasks in AVAX, answering who an agent is and what its track record says.
Identity and reputation are the discovery half of the problem. They tell an agent which counterparty to pick. But picking is not transacting. Between “I trust this agent” and “the work is done and paid for” sits a settlement event, and that event is the one that has quietly broken the open web for thirty years.
HTTP has had a status code for it since 1997. 402 Payment Required has sat unused the entire time, because there was never a settlement layer cheap and fast enough to invoke it. Card networks have fixed fees that make sub-dollar payments absurd. PayPal and its cousins demand accounts and KYC. First-generation blockchains had fees too high and throughput too low to settle anything granular. So the web aggregated instead: monthly subscriptions, bundles, paywalls. We pay for the month because nobody could bill us for the minute. That is a workaround for a settlement problem, not a design anyone loved.
Agents do not tolerate the workaround, because they do not transact like us.
Why Avalanche Fits Agentic Payments
1. The fee math only works a

76211.subject: OpenAI forces Anthropic to keep Fable
76211.body:
----------
######                  [Collaborate with us](https://utm.genai.works/r/he2t9cz)


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/8776fa6e-e2d0-4aec-9648-1bed6de3a659/GenAI_Daily_Banner.jpg?t=1778687445)
Caption: 


----------

----------
**Welcome back!** OpenAI's new model is so good it forced Anthropic to officially keep Fable in subscriptions after all, a Chinese open model matched Fable and sold out in two days, an autonomous agent broke into Hugging Face over a weekend and Google wiped $200 billion off its own value delaying Gemini.



**In today's Generative AI Newsletter:**

* **Anthropic:** Why can't Anthropic take Fable away anymore?

* **Moonshot:** What model sold out in 48 hours?

* **Hugging Face:** How did one agent breach a whole company?

* **Google:** What cost Google $200 billion in a day?


----------



----------
## [OpenAI forced Anthropic to keep Fable](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/15424964-claude-fable-5-on-your-plan)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/06d2a4dd-db17-4ff5-9e6b-7f3efde318fd/Beehiiv_01_-_Jul_20.png?t=1784541504)
Caption: 

Anthropic made its call on Fable 5 but it's permanent this time. Starting today, Max and Team Premium keep the model at half their usual limits. Pro and Team Standard lose it, with a one-time $100 credit as a parting gift.

That $100 goes fast. At Fable's rates a single heavy session eats the whole thing, so for Pro users the real message is upgrade or drop to a weaker model.

The plan a week ago was to cut Fable from subscriptions entirely and charge by the token. GPT-5.6 Sol killed that idea.

With OpenAI matching Fable on the benchmarks at a third the cost, pulling your best model from the $200 plan hands every wavering subscriber a reason to leave.

So the best model stays, but only for the people paying the most.

We said the goodbye to Fable would be the next hello to Opus 5. Anthropic found a third door. No Opus 5, Fable stays, and the Pro tier keeps it.


----------



----------
## [The AI company Nvidia and Google pay to reach new buyers](https://utm.genai.works/r/lirgco2)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/d9797fc3-421b-4f85-b3c3-8915655fcbb9/ChatGPT_Image_Jul_20__2026__12_58_41_PM.png?t=1784541576)
Caption: 

Anyone can build an AI product now. Getting it in front of real buyers is the hard part, and that's what GenAI Works does.

ToneUp turns data from a 14M+ community into distribution no competitor can reach, which is why 300+ enterprise customers already trust us.

The numbers behind it:

* 2.5x revenue growth last year.

* Backed by 300+ enterprise customers, including Nvidia, Oracle, Google and IBM.
